title: Fitting a gusset panel to a box cushion cover
subtitle: Sew a box cushion cover with a separate gusset strip to give the cushion its full depth
excerpt: How to make and fit a gusset panel to a box cushion cover. The gusset is the side strip that gives a box cushion its depth and square profile. Covers cutting and pinning the gusset, easing it around corners, and finishing the opening seam.
sourceNotes: Upholstery: A Beginner's Guide, Alex Law (2010). City and Guilds Level 2 Upholstery. Traditional UK upholstery trade practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: The strip of fabric that forms the sides and part of the back of a box cushion cover, joining the top panel to the bottom panel and giving the cushion its full three-dimensional depth. Cut to the full perimeter of the cushion (or in sections with seams at the corners). On a cushion with a zip opening, the gusset is typically made in two parts: a continuous front section and a zip section across the back.

Making a series of short cuts into the seam allowance at a corner to allow the fabric to splay open and lie flat around the corner without puckering. On an internal (concave) corner, clip diagonally into the seam allowance almost to the stitch line. On an external (convex) corner, notch a small triangle out of the seam allowance. Clipping is done after stitching and before turning.

content.text: A box cushion has three panels: a top, a bottom, and a
that gives the cushion its depth. The gusset must ease around the corners of the top panel. The seam allowance at each corner needs
so the corners sit square when turned and filled. This tutorial covers a rectangular cushion with a continuous gusset and a zip opening at the back.

Upholstery fabric. A sewing machine with an upholstery or denim needle (size 16 or 18). Thread matching the fabric. Upholstery shears. A tape measure. Pins. A zip (if using a zip opening); length equal to the back edge of the cushion less 50 mm. An iron for pressing seams flat.

Measure the foam insert: length, width, and depth. Add 15 mm seam allowance to all dimensions. Cut: one top panel (length + 30 mm x width + 30 mm). One bottom panel the same dimensions. For the gusset, cut a continuous strip: the gusset width equals the foam depth plus 30 mm (two seam allowances). The gusset length equals the full perimeter of the cushion (2 x length + 2 x width) plus 50 mm for seaming. If using a zip across the back: cut the back gusset section 30 mm wider to allow for the zip installation.
Step 2, pin and stitch the gusset to the top panel

With right sides together, pin the gusset to the top panel: start at the centre of one long edge and work toward the corners. At each corner, stop with the needle down, lift the foot, clip the seam allowance of the gusset to 3 mm from the needle position, and turn the gusset to follow the corner. This clipped relief allows the gusset to pivot without puckering at the corner. Continue pinning to the next corner. Once pinned all around, stitch the seam with a 15 mm seam allowance, easing the corners as you reach each one.
Step 3, clip corners and attach the bottom panel

Clip the seam allowance at each corner to within 3 mm of the stitch line; this allows the corner to open flat when turned. Do not clip the stitch line. Press the seam allowances open with an iron. Attach the bottom panel to the other edge of the gusset in the same way, leaving one back edge open for the zip or hand-stitch opening. Stitch. Clip corners. Press seams.
Step 4, install the zip and finish

For a zip opening: fold the raw edge of the back opening over by 15 mm, press, and stitch the zip in place using a zipper foot on the sewing machine. Open the zip before turning right-side out. Turn the cover right-side out through the opening. Push the corners square from the inside with a blunt object (the handle of the shears). Insert the foam. Close the zip. The corners should sit square with no pulling or puckering.

attrs.symptom: Corners pucker or bubble after turning
attrs.cause: Insufficient clipping at the corner seam allowance.
attrs.fix: Turn inside out again, and clip closer to the stitch line at the puckered corner — in 1 mm increments, testing after each clip.

attrs.symptom: Gusset is too short or too long at the back opening
attrs.cause: Gusset cut without accounting for the seam allowances at its own seam joins.
attrs.fix: Add 30 mm (two seam allowances) per join in the gusset length when cutting. If the gusset is already cut too short, add a short extension piece and seam it into a corner position where the join will be invisible.
